questions.question: What is your current citizenship?
questions.helper_text: Your citizenship determines whether you qualify for the E-2 Treaty Investor visa. Only nationals of treaty countries are eligible.
questions.tooltip: E-2 visas are only available to nationals of countries with a Treaty of Commerce and Navigation with the United States.

questions.question: Who is this application for?
questions.helper_text: This determines the scope of your application package, who is included, and pricing.

options.text: I am the sole applicant
I am the principal — my spouse will accompany me as a dependent
My spouse and I are co-investing in this business together
I have a business partner (not my spouse)

questions.question: Will your children also be moving with you?
questions.helper_text: Your response determines dependent visa strategy and pricing.

Q0-02: I am the principal — my spouse will accompany me as a dependent
My spouse and I are co-investing in this business together

options.text: No children
Yes — all under 21
Yes — one or more are 21 or older
options.warning_message: Children 21 or older cannot be E-2 dependents. They will need their own US visa. Only children under 21 will be included in your application.

questions.question: Who is moving with you?
questions.helper_text: Your response determines E-2 dependent visa strategy and pricing.

Q0-02: I have a business partner (not my spouse)

options.text: Just me — I am moving alone
Me and my spouse
Me, my spouse, and our children
Me and my children only

questions.question: What are your children's ages?
questions.helper_text: Dependent status only applies to unmarried children under 21. This affects timeline and compliance planning.

Q0-03: Me, my spouse, and our children

options.text: No children
Yes — all under 21
Yes — one or more are 21 or older
options.warning_message: Children 21 or older do not qualify as E-2 dependents. They will need their own visa pathway.

questions.question: Will you have a business partner or co-investor?
questions.helper_text: Partnership structure affects ownership requirements, treaty basis, and pricing. E-2 principal applicants must own at least 50% of the enterprise.
questions.tooltip: Under 9 FAM 402.9, an equal partnership with more than two investors does not give any individual the control required for E-2 investor classification.

Q0-02: I have a business partner (not my spouse)

options.text: No — I am the only investor
Yes — one partner, we will each own 50%
More than one partner

questions.question: What is your partner's role in the business?
questions.helper_text: Both partners in a 50/50 E-2 application must demonstrate active development and direction of the business.

Q0-04: Yes — one partner, we will each own 50%

options.text: Yes — my partner will actively manage the business with me
No — my partner is a silent investor, not day-to-day involved
options.warning_message: Both partners in a 50/50 E-2 application must demonstrate active development and direction of the business. A silent investor role may not satisfy this requirement for your partner. We recommend reviewing this structure with an attorney before proceeding.

questions.question: Where are you applying from?
questions.helper_text: Your current location determines whether you apply through a US consulate or through USCIS Change of Status.

options.text: From outside the US — consular processing at my home country consulate
From inside the US — I have a valid visa or immigration status
options.warning_message: You may be eligible for Change of Status through USCIS, which avoids a consulate interview. Your case file will include guidance on both pathways.

options.text: From inside the US — I am not sure if my status is currently valid
options.warning_message: If your status has lapsed, applying while out of status creates serious legal risk. Please confirm your status before proceeding.

options.text: From inside the US — I do not have valid status

questions.question: What sources will you use to fund your investment?
questions.helper_text: Select all that apply. The E-2 requires capital that you own and control. Documentation of your source of funds is a critical part of the application.
questions.tooltip: Under 9 FAM 402.9, the investment capital must be at risk and under the investor's control. Loans secured by the business itself do not qualify.

options.text: Personal savings or accumulated wealth
Proceeds from selling property
Proceeds from selling a business or investment
Inheritance or gift received
options.warning_message: Gift-funded investments require documentation proving the gift is genuine — not a disguised loan. The donor's source of funds may also be required.

options.text: Retirement account funds (RRSP, TFSA, pension withdrawal)
A personal loan secured by my own assets (home equity, personal guarantee)
options.warning_message: Personal-asset-secured loans can qualify if the loan is not secured by the business itself. Documentation of the collateral is required.

options.text: A loan secured by the business I am investing in

questions.question: What is your approximate investment range?
questions.helper_text: The E-2 visa requires a substantial investment relative to the total cost of the enterprise. Typical approved investments range from $100,000 to $500,000+.

options.warning_message: Investments below $100,000 face additional scrutiny. Your business plan must clearly demonstrate the investment is substantial relative to the enterprise type.
Investments in this range are borderline for E-2 classification. Consular officers may question whether the investment meets the substantiality requirement.

options.text: Under $50,000

questions.question: What is your current business situation?
questions.helper_text: Your answer helps us tailor your application preparation and connect you with the right resources.

options.text: I have a specific business or franchise identified
I am still searching — I have not chosen yet
I want to start a new business from scratch

questions.question: What type of business are you investing in?
questions.helper_text: Different business types have different E-2 compliance requirements. This determines what documentation your case file will include.

Q0-08: I have a specific business or franchise identified

options.text: A franchise — buying into an established brand
An existing independent business I am acquiring
A new business I am starting from scratch
Professional services or consulting
options.warning_message: Solo consulting businesses face scrutiny under the marginality test. Your business plan must show capacity to generate income well beyond your household needs and to create employment for others.

options.text: Real estate holding, rental property, or passive investment
Non-profit or charitable organisation

questions.question: Would you like us to connect you with an E-2 specialist franchise broker?
questions.helper_text: They work with investors at your profile level and know which businesses have the strongest approval track records at your consulate.

Q0-08: I have a specific business or franchise identified
I am still searching — I have not chosen yet
I want to start a new business from scratch

options.text: Yes — please connect me
No thanks

questions.question: Does any of the following apply to your immigration history?
questions.helper_text: We ask about prior immigration issues to ensure your application addresses them proactively. This is not a disqualifier — transparency strengthens your case.

options.text: No — clean history
Yes — I have something to disclose

questions.question: Have you ever been refused a US visa?
questions.helper_text: Prior visa refusals do not automatically disqualify you, but they must be addressed in your application.

Q0-09: Yes — I have something to disclose

options.text: No
Yes, once — more than 5 years ago
options.warning_message: A prior refusal more than 5 years ago is a manageable risk factor. Your cover letter will address the circumstances of the original refusal.

options.text: Yes, once — within the last 5 years
options.warning_message: A recent visa refusal requires careful preparation. Your case file will need to directly address why the prior application was denied and what has changed.

options.text: Yes, more than once
options.warning_message: Multiple prior refusals indicate a pattern that must be carefully addressed. We recommend consulting with an attorney to assess your options.

questions.question: Have you ever been refused entry at a US border or deported from the US?
questions.helper_text: Entry refusals and removals are serious matters that require careful handling in your application.

Q0-09: Yes — I have something to disclose

options.text: No
Yes — refused entry
options.warning_message: A prior entry refusal at a US border is a significant factor. We recommend speaking with an immigration attorney to address this before proceeding.

options.text: Yes — deported or removed
options.warning_message: A prior deportation or removal creates serious legal complexity. You must consult with a qualified immigration attorney before proceeding with any new application.

questions.question: Do you have any criminal convictions in any country?
questions.helper_text: Criminal history does not automatically disqualify you, but it must be disclosed. Certain convictions may require a legal waiver.

Q0-09: Yes — I have something to disclose

options.text: No
Minor conviction, resolved more than 10 years ago
options.warning_message: An older minor conviction is generally manageable. Your case file will address the nature of the conviction and how long ago it was resolved.

options.text: Minor conviction, within the last 10 years
options.warning_message: A recent conviction requires careful legal analysis. You should consult with an immigration attorney to determine whether a waiver is needed.

options.text: Serious conviction
I am not sure if my record affects US admissibility
options.warning_message: Uncertainty about how a conviction affects US admissibility requires professional legal assessment. We recommend consulting an immigration attorney.

questions.question: What ties do you maintain in your home country?
questions.helper_text: Select all that apply. Strong home ties demonstrate non-immigrant intent — a critical factor in E-2 adjudication.
questions.tooltip: E-2 applicants must demonstrate they intend to return to their home country. The strength of home ties is a key adjudication factor.

options.text: Property I own and plan to keep
Close family remaining in my home country
Active financial accounts, pension, or investments
An ongoing business or professional practice
Professional licences or memberships
None of these apply
options.warning_message: Having no significant ties to your home country is a known risk factor for 214(b) immigrant intent assessment. Your cover letter will need to address this directly and thoroughly. This does not stop your application — it is something we will prepare you for carefully.

hard_stops.PR-NON-TREATY: Your country of citizenship does not currently have an active E-2 treaty with the United States. Only nationals of treaty countries are eligible for E-2 classification.
hard_stops.PR-02: The E-2 requires capital at risk that you own and control. A loan secured by the business itself does not meet this requirement under 9 FAM.
hard_stops.PR-04: Your investment amount appears below the typical threshold for E-2 classification. The treaty investor visa requires a substantial investment relative to the total cost of the enterprise.
hard_stops.PR-LOW-INVESTMENT: Your investment amount appears below the typical threshold for E-2 classification. The treaty investor visa requires a 'substantial' investment relative to the total cost of the enterprise. Amounts below $50,000 rarely meet this standard.
hard_stops.PR-06b: Under 9 FAM 402.9, an equal partnership with more than two investors does not give any individual the control required for E-2 investor classification. Options: restructure to two investors at exactly 50/50 each, or explore the E-2 employee pathway if your company is majority treaty-national owned. E2go prepares investor applications. We recommend consulting an immigration attorney to assess your options.
hard_stops.PR-08: A serious criminal conviction may render you inadmissible to the United States. You must consult with a qualified immigration attorney to determine whether a waiver is available for your situation.
hard_stops.PR-09: Applying while inside the US without valid immigration status creates serious legal risk that could permanently affect your ability to re-enter. Please consult a qualified immigration attorney immediately.
hard_stops.PR-PASSIVE-INVEST: Passive investments such as stock portfolios, rental properties held for income, or other non-operational assets do not qualify for E-2 classification. The investment must be in an active, operating commercial enterprise.
hard_stops.PR-NONPROFIT: Non-profit organisations do not qualify for E-2 treaty investor classification. The E-2 visa requires a for-profit commercial enterprise.

outcomes.PROCEED: Your profile shows strong E-2 eligibility. You meet the core requirements for treaty investor classification.
outcomes.PROCEED_RISK: Your profile shows E-2 eligibility with manageable risk factors. We recommend addressing the flagged items during preparation.
outcomes.ATTORNEY_RECOMMENDED: Your profile has complexity that benefits from legal review. We recommend consulting an immigration attorney alongside your preparation.
outcomes.DO_NOT_PROCEED: Based on your responses, there are significant barriers to E-2 eligibility that should be resolved before proceeding.
